corrections.justification: The Fisher decomposition defines: nominal_10y = real_10y + breakeven_10y. In the TIPS market, the nominal Treasury yield and the TIPS (real) yield are both directly traded and priced by the market independently. Breakeven inflation is derived as the spread between them; it is not a cause of the real yield. If anything, shifts in inflation expectations (breakeven) and real yields are co-determined by monetary policy and macro conditions, but the edge as drawn (real_10y -> breakeven_10y) implies the real yield causes the breakeven, which reverses the more natural interpretation that the nominal yield and breakeven are jointly determined upstream, with fed_funds and macro conditions as the shared drivers. However, since both real_10y and breakeven_10y are independently priced market quantities, the edge between them is more plausibly spurious—both being driven by common upstream factors like fed_funds and cpi_yoy—rather than one causing the other. The edge should be removed as a false positive caused by co-determination from shared upstream variables.

Real TIPS yields and breakeven inflation are both market-priced quantities derived from the same nominal yield curve; they are co-determined by common upstream drivers (fed_funds, cpi_yoy, debt_gdp) rather than one causing the other. The graph already includes fed_funds -> breakeven_10y, cpi_yoy -> breakeven_10y, debt_gdp -> breakeven_10y, and oil_wti -> breakeven_10y as upstream causes of breakeven inflation. Adding real_10y -> breakeven_10y conflates an accounting identity decomposition with a causal mechanism and represents a false positive driven by correlation from shared upstream causes.

The Federal Reserve's policy rate is a primary upstream driver of the entire yield curve, including the real yield on TIPS. When the Fed raises the fed funds rate, short-term real rates rise and this transmits along the curve to raise longer-term real yields (10Y TIPS yields). This well-documented monetary policy transmission channel is currently missing from the graph: fed_funds influences real_10y, which then feeds into gold_price through the opportunity-cost mechanism. This is a false negative.

While VIX and S&P 500 returns are empirically correlated (equity selloffs tend to spike VIX), the causal direction is more complex. VIX is derived from options prices reflecting forward-looking fear, not contemporaneous returns. More importantly, treating sp500_ret as causing vix within the same monthly period creates a problematic simultaneous causality. Both are better understood as co-determined by common upstream shocks (risk sentiment, macro news), and the graph already has vix -> gold_price and sp500_ret -> gold_price as separate pathways. This edge risks introducing a false positive or simultaneity bias into the causal structure.

The causal direction from sp500_ret to mich_pessim is flagged as a bidirectional candidate. Consumer pessimism (mich_pessim) is a forward-looking survey measure of 5-year economic expectations, and it is better understood as a cause or co-determiner of equity market sentiment rather than primarily an effect of monthly stock returns. The Michigan survey captures deep-seated consumer confidence that influences spending and hence corporate earnings expectations. While realized equity returns can update consumer sentiment at high frequency, the 5-year horizon of mich_pessim makes it more of an underlying driver of risk appetite than a reflection of last month's S&P return. However, the contemporaneous monthly feedback is plausible in both directions; the existing direction is defensible and the bidirectional flag is appropriate, so this is a lower-confidence correction.

The unemployment rate affects gold through multiple channels—monetary policy easing expectations, recession-risk safe-haven demand, and risk-aversion—as described in its feature definition. While some of these effects are mediated through fed_funds and vix, rising unemployment also has a direct signal effect on gold demand (flight to safety) that is not fully captured through the mediated paths already in the graph. This direct channel is a false negative given the documented multi-channel relationship between labor market weakness and gold demand.
